title: ii–V–I
desc: The cornerstone of jazz. ii creates tension, V raises it, I resolves.

title: I–VI–ii–V
desc: Classic turnaround moving through the relative minor and subdominant.

title: iii–VI–ii–V
desc: Extended turnaround starting on the mediant. Common in AABA forms.

title: I–IV–iii–VI–ii–V–I
desc: Long-form turnaround through the full diatonic cycle. Heard in standards like Misty.

title: Minor ii–V–i
desc: Dark jazz minor cadence. Half-diminished creates tritone tension, altered dominant resolves.

title: Minor i–VI–ii–V
desc: Minor key turnaround. The bVI gives it its distinctively dark, cinematic quality.

title: Backdoor ii–V
desc: bVII7 approaches I from below — the "backdoor" substitution for the standard V.

title: Tritone Substitution
desc: bII7 replaces V7. The tritone relationship creates a smooth chromatic bass descent.

title: I–II7–V–I
desc: Secondary dominant II7 briefly tonicises V before resolving.

title: Coltrane Changes
desc: Three Imaj7 chords a major 3rd apart, each with its V7. A cycle that shook jazz harmony.

title: Rhythm Changes · A section
desc: I–VI–ii–V in Bb — Gershwin's I Got Rhythm, backbone of bebop for 80+ years.

title: Rhythm Changes · B section
desc: III7–VI7–II7–V7 — a chain of secondary dominants descending the circle of 5ths.

title: Jazz Waltz · I–VI–ii–V
desc: Classic turnaround in 3/4. The lilting feel gives familiar harmony new life.

title: So What / Modal Vamp
desc: i7 with a brief shift to bii7. Floating Dorian quality with no strong resolution.

title: Dorian Vamp · i–IV
desc: Minor i7 to IV7. The major IV chord gives the Dorian mode its characteristic bright colour.

title: Autumn Leaves
desc: ii–V–I–IV descending through the cycle of 4ths. The most used standard template.

title: Misty · opening
desc: Imaj7–bIII7–ii–V. The unexpected bIII7 secondary dominant gives Misty its lush opening.

title: 12-Bar Blues
desc: The foundation. I7–IV7–V7 pattern. Infinite expression.

title: Quick Change Blues
desc: Bar 2 jumps to IV7 then back to I7. Creates extra movement in the first four bars.

title: Minor Blues
desc: The darker sibling. i7–iv7–V7b9 gives a melancholic, soulful quality.

title: Jazz Blues
desc: Blues with jazz ii–V substitutions into IV. The Bird and Monk standard template.

title: 8-Bar Blues
desc: Compact form: I–V–IV–I. Common in country blues and early rock.

title: I–V–vi–IV
desc: The "axis" progression. Underlies hundreds of hit songs across every era of pop music.

title: vi–IV–I–V
desc: Axis starting on the relative minor. Same four chords, distinctly darker feel.

title: I–IV–V
desc: Three chords, a million songs. The backbone of folk, country, and rock n roll.

title: I–vi–IV–V
desc: The "50s progression". Doo-wop, early rock and roll, and countless ballads.

title: I–V–vi–iii–IV
desc: Extended pop loop. The iii minor adds unexpected depth before landing on IV.

title: I–iii–IV–V
desc: The mediant iii creates a flowing, optimistic quality heard in soul and pop classics.

title: I–ii–IV–V
desc: The ii minor adds a jazzy stepping-stone between I and IV. Smooth and melodic.

title: IV–I–V–vi
desc: Axis starting on IV. Opens with an immediate uplifting, anthemic feel.

title: I–bVII–IV
desc: Borrowing bVII from Mixolydian gives rock its raw, anthemic quality. Hendrix to Oasis.

title: i–bVII–bVI–V
desc: Natural minor descent. From Flamenco to metal to film scores — dark and inevitable.

title: i–bVI–bVII–i
desc: Aeolian loop. The bVI and bVII create an open-ended modal quality widely used in rock.

title: I–bVI–bVII–I
desc: Borrowed from parallel minor in a major key. Dramatic and cinematic.

title: I–III7–IV–iv
desc: The chromatic III7→iv colour shift is the neo-soul signature. Rich and sophisticated.

title: I–IV–iii–vi
desc: Stepping down from IV through iii to vi creates warmth and forward motion.

title: ii–IV–I
desc: Bypasses V entirely. Moving ii→IV→I feels lush and inevitable.

title: i–bIII–bVII–IV
desc: Minor with borrowed major chords. Sophisticated, emotionally complex loop.

title: I–bIII–IV
desc: Parallel minor bIII gives funk and soul its gritty edge. Direct and powerful.

title: I–IV Plagal Cadence
desc: The "Amen" movement. Foundational to gospel, blues, and spirituals.

title: I7–IV7–V7
desc: The gospel shuffle. Dominant 7ths throughout give it raw, soulful energy.

title: I–I7–IV–iv
desc: Moving I→I7 tonicises IV, then iv minor adds bluesy colour before returning home.

title: Bossa Nova · I–vi–ii–V
desc: The classic bossa loop. Smooth voice leading, gentle movement, Brazilian jazz.

title: Samba · I–vi–ii–V
desc: Same cycle as bossa but with a driving samba feel. Celebratory and energetic.

title: Flamenco Cadence
desc: i–bVII–bVI–V. Ancient Phrygian descent. Dramatic, cinematic, and deeply emotional.

title: Tango · i–V
desc: Minor-to-dominant oscillation gives tango its tense, seductive quality.

title: i–iv–V–i
desc: Harmonic minor cadence. The raised 7th in V creates its characteristic pull to tonic.

title: Pachelbel Canon
desc: I–V–vi–iii–IV–I–IV–V. One of history's most reused progressions — timeless.

title: Perfect Cadence · V–I
desc: V creates maximum harmonic tension; I resolves it. The most fundamental move in Western music.

title: Deceptive Cadence · V–vi
desc: V resolves to vi instead of I, surprising the listener. Creates forward momentum.

title: Neapolitan · bII–V–I
desc: The Neapolitan chord (bII major) adds a dramatic Romantic-era colour before V–I.

title: I–Vsus4–V–I
desc: The suspended 4th on V delays resolution, creating anticipation. Heard in everything from Bach to pop ballads.

title: I9sus4–I7–IV
desc: The 9sus4 "floating" sound resolving through the dominant 7th to IV. A signature move in soul and gospel.

title: IV9sus4–I–IV9sus4–V
desc: Suspended dominants create an unresolved modal floating quality. Classic Miles Davis electric era.

title: i–Vsus4–V–i
desc: Minor key suspension. The sus4 delay makes the resolution to harmonic minor V feel inevitable.

title: Isus2–I–IVsus2–IV
desc: Sus2 colours give a hazy, impressionistic quality. Widely used in guitar-driven rock and singer-songwriter.

title: I–I+–IV–iv
desc: The tonic augmented chord creates a rising chromatic bass line (R→#5→R of IV). James Taylor, Beatles, bossa nova.

title: I–VI+–ii–V
desc: Augmented VI substitutes for the dominant VI7, creating a chromatic approach into ii. Lush and chromatic.

title: IΔ#5–IV–IΔ#5–V
desc: Maj7#5 as the tonic sound — the Lydian augmented quality. Static, dreamy, and harmonically ambiguous.

title: I6–vi–ii–V
desc: The major 6th tonic is warmer and less static than maj7. Common in older jazz standards and stride piano style.

title: I6/9–IV6/9–ii9–V13
desc: All-extensions progression. 6/9 tonics, a ii9, and a V13 — the fully voiced jazz harmony palette.

title: im6–iv–V7b9–im6
desc: The minor 6 tonic chord (used by Wes Montgomery and Bill Evans) — richer than plain minor, no b7 tension.

title: I–I+–I6–I7
desc: Chromatic voice leading on a static tonic: R→#5→6→b7. Common in slow blues and Ray Charles-style soul.

title: IΔ9#11–V–IΔ9#11
desc: Maj9#11 as a static Lydian tonic. The #11 gives a shimmering, floating quality without strong resolution.

title: IV9#11–I–IV9#11–bVII7
desc: Lydian dominant IV — the #11 lights up the IV chord. Combines with a bluesy bVII7 for fusion colour.

title: Quartal Vamp · i–bii
desc: Two quartal chords a half step apart — the So What sound. Harmonically ambiguous, no strong tonal pull. McCoy Tyner's signature.

title: Quartal landing · IΔ9–V7sus4–IΔ9
desc: Stable extended tonic with a suspended dominant. Pat Metheny and Keith Jarrett territory — diatonic but open-voiced.

title: Quartal blues · I9–IV9sus4–I9–V9sus4
desc: Blues with quartal-flavoured dominant 9th and suspended chords. The Herbie Hancock Maiden Voyage harmonic world.

title: i(Δ7)–im7–iv–V
desc: The classic My Funny Valentine inner-voice descent: Δ7→b7 in the tonic, voice-leading into the subdominant.

title: im(Δ7)–iv9–bVIΔ–V7b9
desc: Minor-major 7th tonic into a dark jazz-minor cadence. Cinematic and emotionally rich.

title: I–I+–IVΔ7–IVm6
desc: The chromatic 5th descent (R→#5→R of IV): I, I+, IVΔ, IVm. Beatles' Michelle, bossa nova, James Taylor.

title: I–I+–IVΔ9–ivm
desc: Same chromatic bass line as above but with extended maj9 tonic landing, giving a richer contemporary feel.

title: IΔ9–iiim9–vim9–bVII9
desc: Smooth descending diatonic + borrowed 9th chords. Every note of movement is a 9th chord — the Wes Montgomery and Mike Stern vocabulary.

title: ii9–V9sus4–IΔ9
desc: Modern jazz landing: suspended V with extensions resolves to a fully voiced tonic. Herbie Hancock and Chick Corea hallmark.

title: IΔ9–iiim7–ii9–V9
desc: Smooth diatonic cycle: tonic→mediant→subdominant→dominant, all voiced with extensions. Sophisticated and forward-moving.

title: im6–bVIIΔ7–bVI6–V7
desc: Minor 6th tonic — a warmer sound with no b7 tension. Through bVII and bVI6 before resolving. Grant Green, Wes Montgomery.

title: I6–IV9–I6–V9
desc: Jazz blues with 6th and 9th dominant chords throughout. The Grant Green and Wes Montgomery blues vocabulary.
